fields.title: CLUSTER SANTE EBOLA- ANALYSE DES GAPS PAR PILIER DE REPONSE AU 11 JUILLET 2026
fields.body:
**RESUME DE L ANALYSE DES GAPS AU 11 JUILLET:**

Au 11 Juillet 2026, 05 provinces affectées (Haut Uele, Ituri, Nord Kivu, Sud Kivu et Tshopo) par la maladie à virus Ebola dans 42 zones de santé .  
L’analyse des écarts (gaps) au 11 Juillet 2026, met en évidence d’importants défis opérationnels liés aux piliers de la réponse dans plusieurs zones de santé touchées par la maladie à virus Ebola.

  
**En Ituri, épicentre de la maladie, avec 26 zones de santé touchées,**

\- 10 zones de santé sont en situation critique, avec un gap supérieur à 86 % (en moyenne 16 piliers non couverts sur 21), à savoir Ariwara, Aungba, Damas, Gety, Kambala,Lolwa, Mandima, Mangala, Nia-Nia et Rimba.   
\- 12 zones de santé affichent un gap supérieur à 66 % (environ 14 piliers non couverts sur 21), notamment Aru, Bambu, Drodro, Fataki, Kilo, Komanda, Lita, Logo, Mambasa, Nizi et Tchomia.  
\- 04 zones de santé présentent un faible niveau de gap (24%) ce qui conrrespond en moyenne à 05 piliers de la reponse non couverts sur 21, il s agit notamment de Bunia, Mongwalu, Nyankunde et Rwampara.

**Au Nord-Kivu, 11 zones de santé touchées,**

\- 03 zones de santé sont dans une situation urgente, avec un gap dépassant 95 % (environ 20 piliers non couverts sur 21) : Kyondo, Masereka et Vuhovi.  
\- 07 zones de santé présentent un gap supérieur à 62 % (environ 13 piliers non couverts sur 21), notamment Beni, Butembo, Kalunguta, Katwa, Mabalako, Musienene et Oicha.   
\- 01 zone de santé, Goma, présente un gap de 28 % (06 piliers non couverts sur 21).

**Au Sud-Kivu, une seule (1) zone de santé est affectée ,**

\- Miti-Murhesa, avec un gap de 57 %, soit 12 piliers non couverts sur 21.

  
**Dans la province de Haut Uele, une seule (1) zone de santé est affectée** ,

\- Wamba, avec un gap de 100 %, soit 21 piliers non couverts sur 21.

  
**Dans la province de Tshopo, 03 zones de santé touchées,**

\- 03 zones de santé sont dans une situation critique et urgente, avec un gap de 100 % ( 21 piliers non couverts sur 21) : Lubunga, Makiso-Kisangani et Mangobo.

En résumé:

Les gaps observés au niveau des piliers de la réponse compromettent significativement l’efficacité globale de la riposte à l’épidémie.  
Ils limitent la capacité à assurer une détection précoce, un isolement rapide, une prise en charge adéquate et des actions de prévention efficaces.  
Ainsi, plus ces insuffisances sont importantes, plus le risque est élevé de voir l’épidémie se prolonger, s’aggraver et se propager vers de nouvelles zones.

NB: les partenaires sont encouragés à partager leur positionnement dans les zones affectées et à risques afin de refléter la réalité des gaps du terrain.

source.spanish_name: Organización Mundial de la Salud

fields.title: TCHAD : Aperçu des conflits inter/intracommunautaires (Janvier _ Juin 2026)
fields.body:
Au premier semestre 2026, un total de 35 conflits ont été enregistrés au Tchad contre 25 au premier semestre 2025, soit une hausse des incidents de 40%. Le bilan humain (81 morts et 167 blessés) est quant à lui en baisse, comparativement à la même période en 2025 (136 morts et 166 blessés). Sur l’année 2025, au total 50 conflits avaient été rapportés pour 200 décès et 273 blésées.  
Les conflits restent concentrés dans certaines zones, avec 37 % des incidents au Lac, 37 % au sud, 23 % à l’est et 3 % au centre, traduisant une répartition géographique étendue des violences.  
Les conflits intercommunautaires dominent largement les incidents (23 cas sur 35, soit 66% des conflits), illustrant une aggravation des tensions entre les communautés. Un conflit intracommunautaire, un conflit agriculteurs - agriculteurs, et un conflit de type religieux ont été également enregistrés au cours de la période sous revue. Enfin, les ressources naturelles demeurent un facteur de conflit, avec 05 cas de conflits agro-pastoraux et 04 cas de conflits liés au foncier.

fields.title: DRC: Ebola BVD Outbreak: LTC Situation Report (Telecoms) #6 (Reporting period: 06 - 12 July 2026)
fields.body:
*This situation report provides updates on the Ebola Bundibugyo Virus Disease (BVD) Outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). It outlines planned and ongoing telecommunications activities of the Logistics & Telecommunications Cluster1 (LTC) Telecoms to support the ongoing humanitarian response.*

**Summary Points**

• The Ebola Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) outbreak in the DRC continues to expand, with more than 1,700 confirmed cases reported as of 09 July. As pressure on surveillance, coordination, and information management increases, reliable telecommunications services remain critical to support an effective response.

• LTC Telecoms and the United Nations Department of Safety and Security (UNDSS) restored the dedicated UN security communications channel in Bunia after six months of downtime and have begun establishing a dedicated channel for NGO partners, strengthening security communications for responders operating across Ituri.

• LTC Telecoms launched a partner connectivity mapping exercise to identify existing and planned satellite connectivity deployments supporting the Ebola response. The initiative will help identify coverage gaps, avoid duplication, and guide the prioritization of telecommunications support in affected areas. While the survey remains openended, partners are encouraged to complete it as soon as possible to support timely analysis and coordination.

fields.title: Regional External Update #39 West and Central Africa Region - Eastern DRC Situation - June 2026
fields.body:
**HIGHLIGHTS**

• The protection environment in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) remained highly fragile, as recurrent armed clashes and rights violations by non-state armed groups continued to drive internal and crossborder displacement. Since January 2026, around 15,700 Congolese refugees and asylum-seekers have fled to neighbouring countries, while the Ebola outbreak declared in May has further deepened humanitarian needs, with the Government currently ,reporting more than 1,900 confirmed cases and 700 deaths.

• Despite the fragile context, large-scale voluntary returns from Burundi continued, with more than 50,200 Congolese returning home through self-organized movements and 8,200 supported through Governmentfacilitated convoys with UNHCR support as of 30 June. However, voluntary repatriation from the DRC to Rwanda remained suspended due to the May border closure linked to Ebola containment measures, leaving around 500 Rwandan refugees stranded in transit centres in Goma and Bukavu.

• In Burundi, UNHCR and the Government completed Level 2 biometric registration at the Busuma site, registering more than 54,300 Congolese refugees and asylum-seekers. Despite ongoing returns, overcrowding at the site strained reception. Critical gaps remained in the provision of shelter, water, sanitation and hygiene, health, education and livelihood services.

fields.title: Statement on the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo by Tom Fletcher, Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs and Emergency Relief Coordinator, 9 July 2026
fields.body:
*9 July 2026*

We need to move faster to break the back of Ebola in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC).

Ituri province remains the centre of the outbreak, but the virus is spreading across other provinces where conflict and the constant movement of people increase the risk of further transmission.

Since the outbreak was declared on 15 May, more than 1,700 people have been infected and 600 have died in DRC. Uganda has confirmed 20 cases. Our colleagues at the World Health Organization and Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention are expertly supporting the epidemiological response under the leadership of the Government.

But this is more than a public health emergency. Before Ebola struck, millions were already facing conflict, hunger, displacement, weak basic services and limited healthcare. DRC is one of the world's most complex humanitarian crises. Recent cuts in humanitarian funding have made the response even harder.

We know how to stop Ebola and we’re implementing our strategy.

The Inter-Agency Standing Committee – the leaders of the world's largest aid organisations – has activated a System-Wide Scale-Up to control the disease in the DRC.

We have deployed a Senior Ebola Coordinator to the outbreak areas to strengthen leadership across the response, support operations and remove bottlenecks.

I have released up to US$60 million from the Central Emergency Response Fund to accelerate the response in the DRC and help preparedness in neighbouring countries at risk – Uganda, South Sudan and Burundi.

We coordinate across borders. We prepare to respond where the outbreak may go next. I remain in constant contact with the UN Resident and Humanitarian Coordinators in the region.

Now we need others to act with the same urgency.

All parties must facilitate safe and sustained access for humanitarian and health workers, supplies and response equipment.

Borders and supply routes must remain open. Closures and blanket travel restrictions disrupt humanitarian operations, health services and essential trade without preventing the spread of disease.

Donors must ensure that pledged funding reaches responders quickly and flexibly. We must support both the Ebola response and the wider humanitarian operation that vulnerable communities depend on.

And now is the time to invest in preparedness. The best defence against Ebola remains strong surveillance, laboratory testing, referral systems, infection prevention and control, and community engagement.

Above all, trust saves lives. Communities must remain the centre of this response. Local leadership, accountability to people, protection against sexual exploitation and abuse, and a response tailored to both women and men must be integral to every part of the operation.

Any delay will be measured in Ebola deaths and in lives lost to the wider humanitarian consequences of this outbreak.

fields.title: Sustaining Protection Under Pressure: The Continuing Impact of Funding Cuts on Protection Programming in West and Central Africa
fields.body: In mid-2025, the Regional Protection Working Group for West and Central Africa (RPWG-WCA) issued an advocacy note documenting the alarming impact of humanitarian funding reductions on protection programming across West and Central Africa. This snapshot provides an update as of June 2026, drawing on a Global Protection Cluster (GPC)-led survey of 258 partner organisations in the region, complemented by operational reporting from national and international NGOs and UN agencies.

fields.title: Ebola outbreak intensifies across DRC as risk of spread to South Sudan grows
fields.body:
- 1,926 and 702 deaths confirmed by Ministry of Health as of 11 July
- 5 provinces are now affected by the outbreak: Ituri, North Kivu, South Kivu, Tshopo and Haut Uele
- Operational constraints continue to limit response implementation, increasing the risk that undetected chains of transmission will continue to spread
- Contact tracing has increased to 78.3%, but remains below the 90-95% target recommended by WHO to contain an outbreak

Kinshasa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, July 13, 2026 — The International Rescue Committee (IRC) warns that the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is worsening on two fronts: transmission is accelerating in locations already affected, while the virus is spreading into new areas, significantly increasing the risk of cross-border spread into South Sudan.

Case numbers have continued to rise steadily, suggesting the outbreak has not yet reached its peak. There are now five provinces in the DRC affected by the Ebola outbreak, including Tshopo province where the town of Kisangani is situated - over 500km west of the current epicentre, Bunia. The Ministry of Health added ten provinces that are now considered at high risk, including Kinshasa, and are targeted with additional protocols and preparedness activities.

*“The risk to South Sudan is particularly alarming. If Ebola crosses the border, it could spread silently before being detected, making the response far more complex and putting countless lives at risk,”* said Bob Kitchen, Vice President of Emergencies for the IRC. *“ Weak surveillance systems, limited health infrastructure, ongoing conflict and a sparse humanitarian presence could delay detection and response.”*

The confirmation of two cases in Wamba, Haut-Uélé Province, near the South Sudan border has significantly heightened the risk of cross-border transmission. The [WHO](https://www.thelancet.com/journals/laninf/article/PIIS1473-3099%2826%2900320-8/fulltext) estimates a 70% likelihood that Ebola will spread into the country. The emergence of cases in Kisangani, is also deeply concerning, as the city sits on the Congo River, a major transport corridor linking eastern DRC with Kinshasa, raising the risk of wider geographic spread.

The IRC is supporting preparedness and response efforts in high-risk areas, strengthening infection prevention and control, surveillance, community engagement, and support for frontline health workers to help contain the outbreak and prevent further spread.

Operational constraints, including border and airport closures and security challenges within the DRC, continue to limit response implementation. With response efforts not yet fully operational across all affected areas, undetected chains of transmission may continue to spread.

source.name: International Rescue Committee
source.spanish_name: Comité Internacional de Rescate

fields.title: UNICEF Cameroon Cholera Outbreak Flash Update No. 1, 3 July 2026
fields.body:
**Overview**

Historical data show that the Far North Region has experienced recurrent cholera outbreaks, with the largest outbreaks recorded in 2010 and 2011. Although recent outbreaks have been smaller in absolute numbers, the current 2026 outbreak is concerning because the case fatality rate of 6.76 per cent is higher than the rates recorded in 2024, 2022, 2019 and 2018, and is close to the high level observed during the major 2010 outbreak.

The current epidemic curve also shows a sharp rise in epidemiological week 26, underlining the need for rapid scaleup of surveillance, early case detection, timely referral and treatment, household disinfection, safe water interventions and community engagement.

Since 23 June 2026, several cases of acute watery diarrhoea (AWD) compatible with suspected cholera were reported in Fotokol, Mada, Makary and Kolofata health districts. Laboratory results confirmed the cholera outbreak in the Far North Region, with four culture-confirmed cases reported as of 29 June 2026. The outbreak is occurring in a cross-border context, with neighbouring Nigeria also affected by a cholera outbreak since May 2026.

The affected health areas are Amchide, Fotokol, Sagme, Bargaram, Hile-Alifa, Biamo and Makary. The cumulative attack rate is 11.84 cases per 100,000 population across the four affected districts. Mada has reported the highest number of cases, while Fotokol has the highest attack rate and case fatality rate.

fields.title: République Démocratique du Congo, Province du Tanganyika : Enregistrements et enquêtes d'intentions personnes déplacées internes, 13 sites dans les zones de santé de Kalemie et Nyemba (10 - 22 juin 2026)
fields.body: Le rapport d’enregistrement et d’enquêtes d’intentions de la DTM dans la province du Tanganyika présente un aperçu de la présence, de la démographie, des vulnérabilités et des intentions de retour des personnes déplacées internes (PDI) enregistrées dans 13 sites du territoire de Kalemie. Les données ont été collectées entre le 10 et le 22 juin 2026, permettant l’enregistrement de 8 331 ménages représentant 18 259 individus. Les déplacements étaient principalement liés aux attaques de groupes armés (51%), aux aléas naturels (24 %) et aux conflits intercommunautaires (19%). La majorité des ménages (83%) ont indiqué leur intention de rester sur leur site actuel au cours des six prochains mois, tandis que 15 pour cent envisagent un retour dans leur zone d’origine. Parmi les besoins prioritaires identifiés pour faciliter des retours sûrs et durables figurent l’accès aux services de base, l’amélioration de la sécurité, les moyens de subsistance et la réhabilitation des logements.

fields.title: Ebola Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak: Democratic Republic of the Congo | Uganda Weekly External Situation Report 08, Data as of 05 July 2026
fields.body:
**Event description**

The Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo continues to intensify, driven by sustained transmission in hotspot health zones of Ituri and North Kivu provinces. The outbreak is marked by growing numbers of community deaths, and the continued spread of infection into previously unaffected health zones. While Uganda has not reported any new confirmed cases during the past week, and the imported case reported in France has fully recovered without evidence of secondary transmission among identified contacts, the ongoing epidemic in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo continues to pose a significant regional and global public health threat.

*Democratic Republic of the Congo*

Compared with the previous update issued on 28 June 2026 (Situation Report #7), the epidemiological situation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo has deteriorated further. An additional 317 confirmed cases and 144 confirmed deaths have been reported, representing increases of 24.3% and 38.2%, in cumulative cases and deaths respectively. The crude case fatality ratio (CFR) rose from 28.8% to 32.1%. Geographic spread continues as the first confirmed case was detected in Lolwa health zone in Ituri Province, increasing the total number of affected health zones to 36.

Mabalako and Vuhovi health zones have now completed 21 consecutive days, without reporting a confirmed case and have been added to the list of areas with no recent transmission. However, Rimba Health Zone, which had previously gone 25 days without reporting a case, has now reported a new confirmed case. This brings the total number of previously affected health zones that have surpassed the 21-day threshold to eight. These include MitiMurhesa in South Kivu Province (46 days); Gety (45 days), Mambasa (33 days), and Aru (31 days) in Ituri Province; and Kalunguta (42 days), Goma (41 days), Vuhovi (24 days), and Mabalako (22 days) in North Kivu Province.

The outbreak remains active in 28 health zones across Ituri and North Kivu provinces that have reported confirmed cases within the past 21 days. During this period, 787 confirmed cases and 325 confirmed deaths were reported. Ituri Province continues to bear the overwhelming burden of the outbreak, accounting for 710 cases (90.2%) and 282 deaths (86.8%) across 21 active health zones. The remaining seven active health zones in North Kivu reported 77 cases (9.8%) and 43 deaths (13.2%).

The highest transmission was recorded in Bunia (237 cases), Rwampara (190 cases), Mongbwalu (103 cases), Nizi (62 cases), Nyankunde (51 cases), and Lita (24 cases) in Ituri Province. In North Kivu Province, Katwa (27 cases) and Butembo (21 cases) remained the main areas of transmission. Collectively, these health zones accounted for 90.9% of all confirmed cases reported during the past 21 days. A similar pattern was observed for mortality. Bunia reported the highest number of deaths (101), followed by Mongbwalu (66), Rwampara (45), Katwa (22), Nizi (18), Nyankunde (15), Lita (12), Mangala (11), and Butembo (11). Together, these health zones accounted for 92.6% of all confirmed deaths reported during the same period.

The crude CFR of the outbreak also increased over recent weeks. In Ituri Province, the CFR rose from 20.5% on 15 June to 29.7% on 05 July 2026, while North Kivu continued to record the highest provincial CFR, remaining above 56% throughout the reporting period. The largest increase in CFR was observed in Lita Health Zone (+36.4 percentage points), followed by Komanda (+22.1 percentage points), Nizi (+18.1 percentage points), and Bunia (+17.6 percentage points). These elevated CFRs likely reflect continued delays in case detection and healthcare-seeking behaviour, compounded by the persistently high proportion of deaths occurring in the community.

Since the beginning of the outbreak, the Democratic Republic of the Congo has reported 1624 confirmed cases, including 521 confirmed deaths (CFR 32.1%\]. Ituri Province remains the epicentre of the outbreak, accounting for 90.9% (1477) of all confirmed cases and 84.3% (439) of all reported deaths nationwide. The most affected health zones are Bunia (452 cases, 121 deaths), Rwampara (349 cases, 72 deaths), Mongbwalu (288 cases, 149 deaths), Nyankunde (96 cases, 19 deaths), Nizi (78 cases, 19 deaths), Lita (33 cases, 12 deaths), and Mangala (33 cases, 14 deaths), all located in Ituri Province, as well as Katwa (52 cases, 38 deaths), Butembo (40 cases, 18 deaths), and Beni (29 cases, 17 deaths) in North Kivu Province. Together, these health zones account for nearly 89.3% of all confirmed cases and 91.9% of confirmed deaths reported nationally.

Investigation of 430 confirmed deaths as of 05 July 2026, showed that 397 (92.3%) occurred in the community or before admission to a treatment facility, highlighting persistent delays in case detection, referral, isolation, and access to clinical care. Only 33 deaths (7.7%) occurred after patients had been admitted to treatment centres or healthcare facilities.

As of 05 July 2026, a total of 12412 contacts were under follow-up of whom 9624 (77.5%) were successfully seen within the previous 24 hours. Ituri Province accounted for the majority of contacts under follow-up, with 9757 contacts, including 7574 (77.6%) seen during the reporting period. In North Kivu, 2050 out of 2655 contacts (77.2%) were followed up, while all contacts in South Kivu had completed the required 21-day monitoring period. Although contact tracing performance has improved overall, follow-up remains below optimal levels, leaving a significant proportion of contacts unreached and increasing the likelihood of undetected infections and continued transmission.

The proportion of new confirmed cases identified among registered contacts increased steadily as the outbreak progressed, exceeding 40% by late June 2026. Overall, 32.4% of confirmed cases were detected through contact follow-up. However, a substantial number of infections continued to occur outside known contact lists, indicating ongoing gaps in surveillance. These gaps are likely driven by insecurity in affected areas, population displacement and mobility, delayed case detection, community resistance, incomplete epidemiological investigations, and the movement of suspected cases and deceased individuals across affected areas.

source.name: World Health Organization
source.spanish_name: Organización Mundial de la Salud

fields.title: Cameroon: Violence Against Health Care in Conflict 2025
fields.body:
**Repeated Violence and Insecurity Continued to Affect the Functioning of Health Services in Cameroon in 2025**

**Safeguarding Health in Conflict Coalition (SHCC)**  
  
Using data collected by Insecurity Insight, the Safeguarding Health in Conflict Coalition (SHCC) identified [**30 incidents**](https://us.list-manage.com/kZ1NWPKHpQU?e=e2fc04a6ae&c2id=2821d00d2df9cfdc1e0140433e086a6f)of violence against or obstruction of health care in Cameroon in 2025. 18 health workers were kidnapped and nine arrested.  
  
The report examines patterns of attacks affecting health workers, facilities, patients, and transport amid intensifying conflict between armed groups, and explores the consequences for access to health care, particularly in remote and conflict-affected regions. The report notes that the true scale of attacks is likely higher than documented due to underreporting in inaccessible areas and reduced humanitarian presence, including the impact of cuts to USAID funding on health service provision and monitoring.  
  
The report forms part of SHCC's annual report[**'Care in the Crosshairs'**](https://us.list-manage.com/98lZGhuUTYe?e=e2fc04a6ae&c2id=2821d00d2df9cfdc1e0140433e086a6f).

**Key findings:**

- For the second consecutive year, reported health worker kidnappings in Northwest region increased, with Ambazonian separatists and Bui Unity Warriors often named as perpetrators.
- Gendarmerie forces in Northwest and Southwest regions continued to arrest health workers, accusing them of links to opposition groups. Health workers were mostly harmed while on their way to or from work, at home, or pursuing a private activity, highlighting how their vulnerability is heightened in their non-working lives within the community.
- Lockdowns enforced by non-state armed groups and movement restrictions linked to armed clashes limited health workers’ and patients’ ability to travel to obtain health care and disrupted routine preventive health care activities. Insecurity, movement restrictions, and access constraints during lockdown periods forced humanitarian partners to suspend or adapt field operations, resulting in operational delays and reduced capacity to implement activities in certain areas and constraining the delivery of medical assistance.

**Full report available in** [**English**](https://us.list-manage.com/KZcM8JZUSWN?e=e2fc04a6ae&c2id=2821d00d2df9cfdc1e0140433e086a6f) **and** [**French**](https://us.list-manage.com/x_FFPulzPw5?e=e2fc04a6ae&c2id=2821d00d2df9cfdc1e0140433e086a6f)**.**   
  
Insecurity Insight updates data continuously and numbers may change if new information is made publicly available. For the latest available figures, see Insecurity Insight’s [**global map**](https://us.list-manage.com/w97MlyjO4uB?e=e2fc04a6ae&c2id=2821d00d2df9cfdc1e0140433e086a6f) on attacks on health care since 2016.

**Other resources**

Drawing on a decade of systematically collected data, [**Still Under Attack**](https://us.list-manage.com/blPCZ9EF8nu?e=e2fc04a6ae&c2id=2821d00d2df9cfdc1e0140433e086a6f)documents the scale, patterns, and consequences of attacks on health care in conflict-affected settings worldwide since the adoption of UN Security Council Resolution 2286 in 2016:

Based exclusively on Insecurity Insight’s data collection, it highlights how violence against health care is becoming normalised, with serious consequences for civilians, disease response and health systems.

The report provides detailed analysis of perpetrators, methods of attack and emerging risks, including the growing role of new technologies in conflict environments. It also offers recommendations for governments, international organisations, humanitarian actors and the diplomatic community aimed at strengthening the protection of health care and improving accountability for violations.

Published every two weeks, the [**Attacks on Health News Brief**](https://us.list-manage.com/VxwvZD64R_g?e=e2fc04a6ae&c2id=2821d00d2df9cfdc1e0140433e086a6f) comprises threats and violence as well as protests and other events affecting the delivery of and access to health care around the world.

fields.title: Multi-Country Bundibugyo Ebola Virus Disease Outbreak Situation Report No. 8 (29 June - 5 July 2026)
fields.body:
According to the World Health Organization, as of 4 July, the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) has confirmed more than 1,500 cases of Ebola disease caused by the Bundibugyo virus (BVD). As of 5 July, Uganda has confirmed 20 cases, with no new cases during the reporting period.

Cross-border movements, displacement and population mobility are shaping disease transmission, requiring an urgent and rapid response to save lives and protect people on the move, while mitigating regional health risks. Given the unfolding public health risks, IOM’s preparedness and response efforts focus on supporting cross-border communities, points of entry (PoEs) and points of control (PoCs) to inform and guide broader public health interventions.

In line with the Inter-Agency Standing Committee (IASC) system-wide scale-up activation for infectious disease events, IOM is scaling up its response, enabling enhanced surge support, operational capacity and coordination across affected countries.  
IOM’s response continues to focus on Priority 1a countries, DRC and Uganda, where active outbreak response operations are ongoing, and Priority 1b countries, Burundi, Rwanda and South Sudan, which face an elevated risk of cross-border transmission.  
Preparedness interventions are also being implemented in other at-risk countries across the region, including Priority 2 countries,  
Angola, the Central African Republic, Ethiopia, Kenya, the Republic of the Congo, the United Republic of Tanzania and Zambia, to strengthen readiness and enhance regional health security, while supporting continent-wide efforts, particularly as co-lead of the PoE technical working group at the continental level.

source.name: International Organization for Migration
source.spanish_name: Organización Internacional para las Migraciones

fields.title: République centrafricaine: Plan d'action protection contre l'Exploitation et les Abus Sexuels 2026-2027 (décembre 2025)
fields.body:
La République centrafricaine fait partie des pays présentant un risque élevé d'abus et d'exploitation sexuels (EAS) selon l’indice de l'IASC (2025 IASC Sexual Exploitation and Abuse Risk Overview (SEARO) Index).

L’exploitation et les abus sexuels (EAS) sont des actes éthiquement et juridiquement inacceptables susceptibles d’être commis par le personnel des Nations Unies ou de personnel apparenté, y inclus ceux et celles impliqués dans l’assistance humanitaire, l’aide au développement et le maintien de la paix.

L’expression “exploitation sexuelle” désigne le fait d’abuser ou de tenter d’abuser d’un état de vulnérabilité, d’un rapport de force inégal ou de rapports de confiance à des fins sexuelles, y compris, mais non exclusivement, en vue d’en tirer un avantage pécuniaire, social ou politique.

On entend par “abus sexuel” toute atteinte sexuelle commise par la force ou la contrainte ou à la faveur d’un rapport inégal, la menace d’une telle atteinte constituant aussi un abus sexuel.

Le présent Plan d’action 2026-2027 a été élaboré de façon participative par les membres du Réseau PEAS/RCA lors d’une retraite tenue du 9 au 10 décembre 2025 à Bangui en tenant compte des gaps relevés lors de la mise en oeuvre des précédents plans. Il a pour but de prévenir, atténuer et répondre à l'exploitation et aux abus sexuels (EAS) dans le contexte actuel du nexus (Humanitaire-Développement et Paix) du pays.

Par ailleurs, ce document a été bâti sur le modèle du Mémorandum sur la soumission des plans d'action nationaux 2024 et en conformité avec les cinq priorités stratégiques du cadre des résultats PEAS DCO/OSCSEA, au plan d'accélération PEAS de l'IASC et à la stratégie PEAS de la RCA 2025-2030. Ses  
 indicateurs sont conformes à ceux définis par IASC1 au niveau global.

Le Plan d'action 2026-2027 est sous le leadership du Coordonnateur résident des Nations Unies et Coordinateur humanitaire, appuyé par le Coordinateur inter-agences, avec le soutien de la Task-Force et du réseau interagences PEAS. Il s’agit à travers ce document, d’assurer une approche harmonisée de l'UNCT-HCT en s’appuyant sur l’approche centrée sur les victimes et le mainstraiming de la PEAS dans tous les secteurs et programmes.  
 Le Bulletin du Secrétaire général des Nations Unies sur les PEAS (ST/SGB/2003/13) et d’autres documents pertinents référencés ci-bas2 ont servis de fondements à la conception de ce Plan.

fields.title: Central African Republic: Situation Report No. 75, as of 7 July, 2026
fields.body:
**HIGHLIGHTS**

- Critical humanitarian situation in Am-Dafock following recent clashes
- Heavy rains cause human and property damage in Ngaoundaye
- Rafaï: More than 300 displaced and other vulnerable families receive livestock and poultry

**GENERAL CONTEXT**

Haut-Mbomou Prefecture – South-East

- More than 1,000 people have fled from Zemio to Zapaï in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) out of fear for their safety, following the killing on 27 July of a religious leader who had been actively involved in community reconciliation efforts in the area. An unknown number of residents have also undertaken preventive displacement within Zemio town since the end of June, driven by fears of possible clashes between parties to the conflict. Communities are additionally living in fear of reprisals over alleged collaboration with one or another party to the conflict, limiting opportunities for community engagement activities. Furthermore, a local authority was killed by armed men on 5 July along the Dembia axis, while businesses in Zemio town were robbed, further exacerbating the climate of fear affecting the population.

**HUMANITARIAN NEEDS AND RESPONSE**

Vakaga Prefecture – North-East

- On 2 July, humanitarian partners conducted a rapid humanitarian assessment in Am-Dafock, along the border with Sudan, with logistical support from MINUSCA, following the clashes that occurred in the town on 30 June. The assessment found that the humanitarian situation remains critical, with the most urgent needs relating to protection, health care and access to safe drinking water. The town’s only health facility had been looted and was occupied by armed elements at the time of the assessment. The only accessible water point is insufficient to meet the needs of the affected population. In addition, displaced people are unable to leave the site because of security concerns. A MINUSCA helicopter rotation on 4 July enabled the delivery of emergency assistance provided by the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR), International Medical Corps (IMC), the Central African Red Cross and an international orgnaization. The assistance included 425 tarpaulins, 680 buckets, medicines and hospital consumables, personal protective equipment and body bags. IMC is currently operating mobile clinics in Am-Dafock and has already provided essential health services to at least 140 people. However, logistical constraints continue to hamper the scale-up of the humanitarian response, as the main access road has become impassable during the ongoing rainy season. Approximately 24 metric tons of humanitarian cargo remain to be transported, including protection supplies, first-aid kits, soap, gloves and protective masks. With an estimated airlift capacity of two metric tons per helicopter rotation, at least 12 additional rotations will be required to deliver all pending relief items. Consequently, delays in the delivery of humanitarian assistance are expected, which could affect the timely provision of life-saving aid to affected populations.

**Food Security**

Nana-Gribizi Prefecture – Center

- From 22 June to 3 July, Welthungerhilfe (WHH) conducted an agricultural entrepreneurship training program for 230 members from 11 agricultural groups, including 142 women and 88 men. Participants strengthened their skills in business management, planning, marketing, and the organization of agricultural activities. In addition, 37 members of two pastoral groups, including 26 women, received training on good livestock management practices, with a focus on animal nutrition, animal health, and reproduction. To further improve agricultural production practices, 166 lead farmers and technical focal points were trained in Kaga-Bandoro, Ouandago, and Mbrès. The training sessions covered good agricultural practices as well as crop-specific technical production packages tailored to several priority crops.

fields.title: République centrafricaine : Rapport de situation N°75, au 7 juillet 2026
fields.body:
**FAITS SAILLANTS**

- Réponse à la situation humanitaire critique à Am-Dafock à la suite des récents affrontements
- Des pluies diluviennes causent des dégâts humains et matériels à Ngaoundaye
- Rafaï : plus de 300 familles déplacées et autres personnes vulnérables reçoivent du bétail et de la volaille

**CONTEXTE GENERAL**

**Préfecture du Haut-Mbomou – Sud-Est**

- Plus de 1 000 personnes se sont déplacées de Zemio vers Zapaï en République démocratique du Congo (RDC) par crainte pour leur sécurité, suite au meurtre le 27 juillet d’un leader religieux engagé dans des efforts de réconciliation communautaire dans la région. D’autres habitants dont le nombre demeure inconnu se déplacent préventivement depuis la fin juin à l’intérieur de la ville de Zemio, craignant d’éventuels affrontements entre parties au conflit. Les communautés vivent également dans la peur de répressions pour une présumée collaboration avec l’une ou l’autre des parties au conflit, ce qui empêche des activités d’engagement communautaire. Par ailleurs, une autorité locale a été tuée le 5 juillet par des hommes armés sur l’axe Dembia et des commerces ont été braqués dans la ville de Zemio, exacerbant la peur dans laquelle la population vit.

**BESOINS ET REPONSE HUMANITAIRE**

**Multisectoriel**

Préfecture de la Vakaga – Nord-Est

- Les partenaires humanitaires ont mené le 2 juillet, avec l’appui logistique de la MINUSCA, une évaluation humanitaire rapide à Am-Dafock, dans la zone frontalière avec le Soudan, à la suite des affrontements sur-venus le 30 juin dans la localité. Les résultats de cette évaluation indiquent que la situation humanitaire de-meure critique, les besoins les plus urgents concernant la protection, la santé et l’accès à l’eau potable. Le seul centre de santé de la ville a été pillé et était occupé par des éléments armés au moment de l’évaluation. L’unique point d’eau accessible ne permet pas de répondre aux besoins de la population affec-tée. En outre, les personnes déplacées ne peuvent pas quitter le site en raison de préoccupations liées à leur sécurité. Une rotation d’hélicoptère de la MINUSCA effectuée le 4 juillet a permis l’acheminement d’une assistance d’urgence fournie par le Haut-Commissariat des Nations Unies pour les réfugiés (HCR), International Medical Corps (IMC), la Croix-Rouge Centrafricaine (CRCA) et une organisation internationale. Cette assistance comprenait notamment 425 bâches, 680 seaux, des médicaments et des consommables pour l’hôpital, du matériel de protection individuelle et des sacs mortuaires. Actuellement, IMC conduit des cliniques mobiles à Am-Dafock ayant déjà fourni des soins de santé essentiels à au moins 140 personnes. Toutefois, des contraintes logistiques continuent d’entraver l’intensification de la réponse humanitaire, la principale route étant impassable suite à la saison des pluies en cours. Environ 24 tonnes de cargaison humanitaire restent à acheminer, notamment du matériel de protection, des trousses de premiers secours, des savons, des gants et des masques de protection. Avec une capacité de transport estimée de deux tonnes par rotation d’hélicoptère, au moins 12 rotations supplémentaires seront nécessaires pour acheminer l’ensemble des articles de secours en attente. Par conséquent, des retards dans la fourniture de l’assistance humanitaire sont prévisibles, ce qui pourrait compromettre la fourniture en temps opportun d’une aide vitale aux populations affectées.

**Sécurité alimentaire**

Préfecture de la Nana-Gribizi – Centre

- Du 22 juin au 3 juillet, Welthungerhilfe (WHH) a organisé une formation en entrepreneuriat agricole au profit de 230 membres issus de 11 groupements agricoles, dont 142 femmes et 88 hommes. Les participants ont renforcé leurs capacités en gestion, planification, commercialisation et organisation des activités agricoles. En outre, 37 membres de deux groupements pastoraux, dont 26 femmes, ont été formés aux bonnes pra-tiques d’élevage, notamment en matière d’alimentation, de santé animale et de reproduction. Afin d’améliorer les pratiques de production agricole, 166 référents techniques ont bénéficié d’une formation à Kaga-Bandoro, Ouandago et Mbrès. Les sessions ont porté sur les bonnes pratiques agricoles ainsi que sur les itinéraires techniques adaptés à plusieurs cultures prioritaires.

fields.title: Ebola Outbreak: Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda - External update #3 - 10 July 2026
fields.body:
**Highlights**

• The seventeenth Ebola virus disease outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) continues to evolve, with 1,792 confirmed cases, including 625 deaths and 295 recoveries, reported across 37 health zones in Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu as of 8 July. The Government has also reported two confirmed cases in Kisangani, Tshopo Province.

• The outbreak is unfolding in an area marked by persistent insecurity and displacement due to armed clashes, limiting humanitarian access and complicating Ebola prevention, surveillance and response. Ituri remains the epicentre, accounting for 91 per cent of confirmed cases and nearly 86 per cent of deaths.

• While no Ebola cases have been reported among refugees in the DRC to date, 19 internally displaced persons (IDPs) from the Kpangba, Kigonze and Bembeyi sites in Ituri are among confirmed cases. More than 2 million forcibly displaced people live in Ebola-affected or high-risk areas of eastern DRC, including 320,000 refugees and asylum-seekers, heightening the urgency of inclusive preparedness, risk communication, surveillance and access to national response systems.

• In Uganda, the outbreak remains relatively contained, with 20 confirmed cases as of 9 July, including two deaths, 17 recoveries and one patient currently admitted for care.

• UNHCR is supporting the Governments of the DRC and Uganda, alongside the World Health Organization (WHO), other United Nations agencies and local communities, to strengthen Ebola prevention and response and ensure that refugees, asylum-seekers, IDPs and host communities can access information, surveillance and essential services.

fields.title: Understanding Ebola risk in Ituri's IDP sites : perceptions, prevention constraints and response needs
fields.body: This study analyses the perceptions of populations living in displacement sites in Ituri Province regarding the resurgence of Ebola Virus Disease (EVD), the effects of the outbreak on their living conditions, and the main needs expressed to strengthen prevention and response efforts in the sites. The findings indicate that, according to the key informants interviewed, displaced people have a high level of risk perception in relation to EVD and largely perceive Ebola as a real and dangerous disease. Knowledge of the main signs of the disease and preventive measures is generally good, particularly in sites already affected by Ebola cases, where respondents also report more frequent exposure to awareness-raising activities and better knowledge of specialised treatment facilities. However, gaps remain in relation to mechanisms for reporting suspected cases. Several information needs were also identified, particularly around better distinguishing the early signs of Ebola from other common illnesses, understanding treatment pathways, and knowing what steps to take when faced with a suspected case. Community health workers, displacement site committees and humanitarian organisations appear to be the actors with the highest levels of trust when it comes to communicating about Ebola. When suspicious symptoms appear, self-medication remains the first response considered, ahead of seeking care from health facilities. Meanwhile, fear of dying in treatment centres and concerns about stigmatisation constitute the main barriers to the early reporting of cases. The study also shows that the resurgence of Ebola is occurring in a context of pre-existing vulnerability and is tending to exacerbate social tensions and everyday hardship in displacement sites. Respondents reported increased fear and mistrust, reduced social interaction, movement and community gatherings, as well as a deterioration in already precarious living conditions. Difficulties in accessing essential services mainly relate to fuel, food, safe drinking water, sanitation facilities, protection and healthcare, with women consistently appearing to be more affected than men. Suspected cases and survivors are perceived as the groups most exposed to stigmatisation. Some households are also considering leaving the sites because of the perceived risk of infection. The study further highlights that handwashing facilities, although present in almost all sites, are widely considered insufficient and are often irregularly supplied with water and soap. In addition, despite the resurgence of Ebola, more than four in five displaced households reported that they had received no humanitarian assistance, both in affected and non-affected sites. Existing interventions are considered insufficient and only partially responsive to the needs expressed, which primarily relate to food assistance, cash transfers, livelihoods, water, hygiene and sanitation, essential household items and shelter. The study shows that the resurgence of Ebola is unfolding in a context of already severe vulnerability in displacement sites. The main challenges go beyond disease prevention alone and point to the need for an integrated response combining risk communication and rumour management, improved early reporting, strengthened WASH infrastructure, access to essential services, protection for women and girls, and multisectoral humanitarian assistance, while also extending preventive interventions to sites that have not yet been affected.

fields.title: Sites de déplacés face à Ebola en Ituri : perceptions du risque, contraintes de prévention et besoins opérationnels (Juillet 2026)
fields.body: Cette étude analyse les perceptions des populations vivant dans des sites de déplacés de la province de l’Ituri face à la résurgence de la Maladie à Virus Ebola (MVE) et les effets de l’épidémie sur leurs conditions de vie, ainsi que les principaux besoins exprimés pour renforcer la prévention et la riposte dans les sites. Les résultats montrent que, selon les informateurs clés interrogés, les personnes déplacées présentent un niveau élevé de perception du risque lié à la MVE et perçoivent majoritairement Ebola comme une maladie réelle et dangereuse. Les connaissances des principaux signes de la maladie et des mesures de prévention sont globalement bonnes, particulièrement dans les sites déjà affectés par des cas d’Ebola, où les répondants rapportent également une exposition plus fréquente aux activités de sensibilisation et une meilleure connaissance des structures spécialisées de prise en charge. Toutefois, des insuffisances persistent concernant les mécanismes de signalement des cas suspects, tandis que plusieurs besoins d'information sont exprimés, notamment pour mieux distinguer les premiers signes d'Ebola des autres maladies fréquentes, comprendre les modalités de prise en charge et les conduites à adopter face à un cas suspect. Les relais communautaires, les comités de déplacés et les organisations humanitaires apparaissent comme les acteurs bénéficiant du plus haut niveau de confiance pour communiquer sur Ebola. Face à l'apparition de symptômes suspects, l'automédication demeure le premier recours envisagé, devant le recours aux structures de santé, tandis que la peur de mourir dans les centres de traitement et la crainte de la stigmatisation constituent les principaux freins au signalement précoce des cas. L'étude montre également que la résurgence d'Ebola s'inscrit dans un contexte de vulnérabilité préexistante et tend à accentuer les tensions sociales et les difficultés quotidiennes dans les sites de déplacés. Les répondants rapportent une augmentation de la peur et de la méfiance, une réduction des interactions sociales, des déplacements et des rassemblements communautaires, ainsi qu'une dégradation de conditions de vie déjà précaires. Les difficultés d'accès aux services essentiels concernent principalement le combustible, la nourriture, l'eau potable, les installations sanitaires, la protection et les soins de santé, les femmes apparaissant systématiquement plus affectées que les hommes. Les cas suspects et les survivants sont perçus comme les groupes les plus exposés à la stigmatisation. Une partie des ménages envisage par ailleurs de quitter les sites en raison du risque perçu de contamination. L'étude met également en évidence que les dispositifs de lavage des mains, bien que présents dans la quasi-totalité des sites, sont largement jugés insuffisants et souvent irrégulièrement approvisionnés en eau et en savon. Aussi, malgré la résurgence d'Ebola, plus de quatre ménages déplacés sur cinq déclarent n'avoir reçu aucune assistance humanitaire, autant dans les sites déjà affectés que ceux qui ne le sont pas. Les interventions existantes sont jugées insuffisantes et ne répondent que partiellement aux besoins exprimés, qui concernent prioritairement l'assistance alimentaire, les transferts monétaires, les moyens d'existence, l'eau, l'hygiène, l'assainissement, les articles ménagers essentiels et les abris. L'étude montre que la résurgence d'Ebola intervient dans un contexte de vulnérabilité déjà très marqué dans les sites de déplacés. Les principaux défis dépassent la seule prévention de la maladie et soulignent la nécessité d'une réponse intégrée combinant communication sur les risques et lutte contre les rumeurs, amélioration du signalement précoce, renforcement des infrastructures WASH, accès aux services essentiels, protection des femmes et des filles et assistance humanitaire multisectorielle, tout en étendant les interventions préventives aux sites non encore affectés.

fields.title: DR Congo: Ebola outbreak a crisis within a neglected crisis | NRC
fields.body:
Statement by Eric Batonon, the Norwegian Refugee Council’s (NRC) country director in DR Congo on the ongoing Ebola outbreak:

 Published 10. Jul 2026

“The current Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak is unfolding in communities already exhausted by years of conflict and repeated displacement.

“In DR Congo’s Ituri province – the current epicentre – displaced communities have faced years in shelters designed for temporary stay, with poor sanitation and little access to healthcare. As armed groups have fought for control of land and valuable minerals, donors and diplomats have often looked the other way and so abandoned millions to lives dominated by repeated displacement and deprivation. This is the context in which the current Ebola outbreak has taken hold, and in part why case numbers continue to rise rapidly.

“Infection prevention and control measures have a vital role to play in the response. Communities must be placed at the heart of all response plans, and direct engagement and trust building remain the cornerstones of any successful strategy. Ebola can only be stopped when communities’ priorities dictate policy.

“In this regard, NRC is currently stepping up its Ebola response, with public engagement activities and community prevention measures. Through the establishment of handwashing stations and the distribution of hygiene products, NRC teams in Ituri are working to bolster infection prevention measures. In addition, our staff are working to prevent disruption to children’s learning and ensure that they can continue to access education in safe conditions. NRC staff are also working to assess the impact of this epidemic on the millions of internally displaced persons (IDPs) in Ituri as well as North and South Kivu provinces. This will help to better shape the response for communities that currently live in IDP sites, often in crowded conditions with poor sanitation.

“DR Congo has appeared on NRC’s annual list of top neglected displacement crisis every year in the last decade. And the international community continues to neglect the humanitarian situation in eastern DR Congo, even as international companies grow rich from its resources. It is now essential that donors ramp up efforts to ensure that the response to Ebola is able to move faster than the disease itself. Flexible and sustained funding and political engagement are vitally important, not only to support those caught in this outbreak but also to meet the pre-existing needs which have contributed to the severity of this epidemic."

**Notes to editors:**

- DR Congo has featured on NRC’s neglected displacement crises list for ten consecutive years ([NRC](https://reliefweb.int/news/2026/dr-congo-on-worlds-most-neglected-displacement-crises-list-for-tenth-consecutive-year)).
- The humanitarian response plan in DRC is currently 53.9 per cent funded with 756.1 million of the 1,403.7 million US dollars required having been delivered ([FTS OCHA](https://fts.unocha.org/plans/overview/2026)).
- The 2025 humanitarian response plan in DRC was just 27.7 per cent funded, while the 2024 plan was 51.4 per cent funded ([FTS OCHA](https://fts.unocha.org/plans/overview/2025)).
- As of the end of 2025, 4.3 million people were internally displaced in DRC. 932,000 displacements were recorded in the Ituri province throughout the year ([IDMC](https://www.internal-displacement.org/countries/democratic-republic-of-the-congo/)).

**For more information or to arrange an interview, please contact:**

- NRC global media hotline: media@nrc.no, +47 905 62 329

fields.title: Rejection and fatigue: Healthcare workers report from the Ebola frontlines - CARE
fields.body:
## The Ebola outbreak in Democratic Republic of Congo continues to spread as healthcare workers across Ituri, North Kivu, and South Kivu provinces race to contain the virus.

Healthcare workers in DRC are reporting growing challenges on the frontlines of the Ebola response. CARE is currently working alongside local partners and has deployed more than 500 trained community health workers in the country. But global funding cuts have severely weakened DRC’s health system and the humanitarian response infrastructure. This reduction in aid funding is also hurting local protective measures against the disease.

Along with the threat of Ebola, teams in some areas are being met with resistance and misinformation in communities. Health workers in Ituri and North Kivu are now sharing the challenges they are encountering in the field.

Nyabatsi Kito Feza is a community health worker trained by CARE. She is in the North Kivu province, and speaks about the challenges she’s facing.

“As community health workers, we faced rejection, mistrust, and accusations while raising awareness about Ebola. Many people refused to let us into their homes, believing we were spreading lies for money. We understand why people who feel abandoned feel this way. We continued to share life-saving messages with those more open to listening, trusting that informed neighbors and friends would help spread the truth too. During previous Ebola outbreaks, including the COVID-19 pandemic, many households were reluctant to engage with us. However, during this 17th Ebola outbreak, the resistance is even more discouraging. People point at us and say, ‘They’ve started their business of making money again.’ In some homes, residents say they are too busy, telling us, ‘We don’t have time, we need to go and earn a living,’ and ask us to return later. It has become common for families to suddenly appear occupied as soon as they see us approaching. Despite these challenges, we continue our work because we understand why this perception exists when suffering is so deep, and protecting communities begins with rebuilding trust and ensuring people have the information they need to keep themselves and their families safe.”

As of publication, CARE-trained community health workers have reached more than210,000 people across North Kivu and Ituri provinces. But more help is needed. CARE is calling for an immediate increase in financial and technical support to strengthen the aid response in DRC and to address the expansive needs communities face, including local and women-led organizations.

Augustin Akyebe is a project manager with CARE partner, [Kujitegemea Actions ](https://kuardc.org)(KUA), in Sake, North Kivu province. He recently spoke with CARE about how years of conflict and crises are wearing down communities.

“The challenge we currently face is community resistance and skepticism toward Ebola prevention efforts. In Sake, where we are working at the moment, many people believe the disease is no longer a threat. This perception is reinforced by misinformation, rumors, and the fatigue communities have experienced after years of conflict and repeated humanitarian crises. This situation has several direct consequences for our work. First, many people become less willing to follow preventive measures such as regular handwashing, avoiding unnecessary physical contact, and reporting symptoms early. Without visible cases, it becomes difficult to convince communities that prevention is still necessary.

Second, our teams sometimes struggle to gain trust. Some community members perceive awareness campaigns as exaggerated or even question the intentions of humanitarian organizations. As a result, we invest more time in community dialogue, listening to people’s concerns, correcting misinformation, and rebuilding confidence. Finally, Sake remains highly vulnerable because it is an important transit hub connecting Goma, Bukavu, Walikale, Rutshuru, Lubero, and other areas. Continuous population movement, including travel from regions where Ebola cases have been reported, increases the risk of introducing the virus. If community vigilance declines, detecting and containing a potential outbreak would become much more difficult.”

**How are communities responding to Ebola prevention and response efforts, and what are their main concerns?**

“In Sake, where communities have endured years of armed conflict and humanitarian emergencies, reactions to Ebola prevention efforts are mixed. While many people recognize the importance of preparedness, others remain skeptical because no confirmed cases have been reported locally. The memory of previous Ebola outbreaks, combined with ongoing insecurity, has created both caution and fatigue. One of the main concerns is the limited availability of preventive infrastructure. Handwashing stations and hygiene supplies are generally available in health facilities, but many public places, markets, and community gathering points still lack adequate prevention measures. Communities are also worried about the economic consequences of public health measures. Border restrictions and movement controls, particularly along the DRC–Rwanda border, have disrupted trade and reduced household incomes, placing additional pressure on already vulnerable families.

Despite these challenges, there are many encouraging signs. Community health workers and local volunteers actively conduct awareness sessions in the Kimoka, Kaduki, and Sake Afya health areas, promoting hand hygiene, early reporting of symptoms, and other preventive practices. However, pockets of resistance remain. Some people, exhausted by years of crisis, continue to view Ebola interventions with suspicion and believe that the disease primarily benefits humanitarian organizations. Addressing these perceptions requires continuous dialogue, transparency, and community engagement.”

[*CARE started working in DRC in 1979*](https://www.care.org/our-work/where-we-work/democratic-republic-of-the-congo/)*. Prior to 1998, CARE worked in the DRC to support Rwandan refugees who had fled there from their own country. In 2002, CARE returned to the DRC to help people affected by the country’s own long-standing conflict.*

*CARE’s programmatic work in the DRC focuses on humanitarian response, women’s economic empowerment, reproductive health, food and nutrition security, environment, resilience, and the right to a life free from violence.*

fields.title: Central African Republic: Overview of incidents affecting humanitarian workers (January - June 2026)
fields.body:
**CONTEXT**

Since January 2026, 53 incidents have affected humanitarian workers in the Central African Republic. In June, 17 incidents were reported, compared with six in May, representing an 89 per cent increase, linked to the conflict dynamics in the south-east and north-east.

14 incidents of criminal nature were reported, including eight armed robberies, three intrusions, two thefts, and one humanitarian aid looting. The remaining incidents involved one illegal taxation, interference with humanitarian activities, and one case of abduction of a humanitarian staff. At least one humanitarian worker was injured.

Birao was the most affected sub-prefecture, with five incidents, followed by Zémio with four incidents. The sub-prefectures of Obo and Bossangoa recorded three incidents each, while two incidents were reported in each of the sub-prefectures of Nana-Bakassa, Bangui-Rapide, Bangui-Fleuve and Ouadda. International NGOs were the most affected, with 10 incidents, followed by national NGOs with six incidents. One United Nations agency was also affected.

Since January 2026, Haut-Mbomou, Bangui, and Vakaga have remained the most affected prefectures, with 14 incidents recorded in Haut-Mbomou and 11 incidents in each of the remaining prefectures.

fields.title: République centrafricaine : Aperçu des incidents ayant affecté les travailleurs humanitaires (janvier-juin 2026)
fields.body:
**CONTEXTE**

Depuis janvier 2026, 53 incidents ont affecté les travailleurs humanitaires en République centrafricaine. En juin, 17 incidents ont été signalés, contre neuf en mai, soit une hausse de 89%, particulièrement liée aux dynamiques du conflit dans le sud-est et dans le nord-est.

14 incidents de nature criminelle ont été rapportés, dont huit braquages à main armée, trois intrusions, deux vols et un pillage de l’aide humanitaire. Les autres incidents comprennent une taxation, une interférence et un cas d’enlèvement d’un acteur humanitaire. Au moins un travailleur humanitaire a été blessé.

La sous-préfecture de Birao a été la plus affectée, avec cinq incidents, suivie de Zémio avec quatre incidents. Les sous-préfectures de Obo et Bossangoa ont enregistré trois incidents chacune, tandis que deux incidents ont été rapportés dans chacune des sous-préfecture Nana-Bakassa, Bangui-Rapide, Bangui-Fleuve et Ouadda. Les ONG internationales sont les plus affectées, avec 10 incidents, suivies des ONG nationales avec six incidents. Une agence des Nations Unies a également été affectée.

Depuis janvier 2026, le Haut-Mbomou, Bangui, et la Vakaga restent les préfectures les plus affectées, avec respectivement 14, et 11 incidents dans chacune des deux dernières préfectures.

fields.title: Acute Humanitarian Response in Education – Learning Review | NRC
fields.body:
Since 2024, NRC has delivered First-line Education Responses in eight crisis-affected countries. The response modality uses Learning through Play to support children to recover from trauma, build relationships, and prepare for continued learning.  
  
**Background**

NRC’s Acute Humanitarian Response in Education, funded by the LEGO Foundation, ensures flexible funds are earmarked for education needs in acute crises. This funding, reserved solely for education responses, has enabled NRC to develop its FLER approach and rapidly deliver critical education support. The funding supports children aged 5-12+ years, as well as the caregivers, families, education practitioners, and community members who care for them, by initiating lifesaving education activities within 42 days. Since 2024, NRC has delivered FLERs in DR Congo, Ethiopia, Lebanon, Palestine, Somalia, Sudan, Syria, and Uganda.

With Learning through Play (LtP) at the center of the approach, NRC FLER seeks to best meet children’s educational, psychosocial, and socialemotional needs in the immediate aftermath of a crisis. Interventions are contextually tailored to maximise children’s improved wellbeing. Programming often includes the establishment of safe and accessible learning spaces, distribution of teaching and learning materials, training for facilators, light school feeding, engagement of parents and caregivers, and LtP sessions for children inclusive of NRC’s flagship Better Learning Programme.

fields.title: Ce que nous disent les données MSNA 2025 sur les besoins humanitaires - Résultats pour Afrique de l’Ouest et Centrale, Juin 2026 | Mali, Burkina Faso, RCA, RDC
fields.body:
**MESSAGES CLÉS**

• Dans les quatre pays d’Afrique de l’Ouest couverts par des évaluations multisectorielles des besoins (MSNA) en 2025, les populations sont confrontées à des crises prolongées et chroniquement sous-financées. Les résultats montrent que les besoins humanitaires y demeurent omniprésents, alimentés par une intensification des conflits armés. En 2025, la dégradation continue de la situation sécuritaire provoque des vagues ininterrompues de déplacements, privant les communautés d’accès à des moyens de subsistance et aux services essentiels, les exposant davantage à des risques de protection.

• La baisse brutale des financements au début de 2025 a particulièrement impacté ce contexte, agrandissant l’écart entre besoins des populations, attention internationale et financement de l’aide. Cela n’a fait que renforcer une tendance déjà présente, le pourcentage de couverture des Plans de Réponse Humanitaire diminuant d’année en année. Le manque de communication autour de ces baisses, ainsi que l’inclusion des communautés dans les processus de décision, sont des retours fréquents des ménages interrogés.

• Malgré des dynamiques communes, chaque pays présente des spécificités et des profils de besoins distincts. En République Centrafricaine et en République Démocratique du Congo, la majorité des ménages cumulent des besoins dans trois secteurs, certains présentant un for tenjeu de santé publique (Eau, Hygiène et Assainissement (EHA), santé, sécurité alimentaire). Au Mali, la plupart des ménages sont dans le besoin dans deux secteurs ou plus, la combinaison la plus courante étant Abri et Biens Non Alimentaires (ABNA)-Education, suivi de ABNA-EHA.

fields.title: Fiche d’information : Accès aux marchés et assistance monétaire | Faisabilité des interventions cash auprès des personnes déplacées en site à Nyemba, République démocratique du Congo (Mai 2026)
fields.body:
**Messages clés**

• L’accès aux marchés reste fortement contraint pour les ménages déplacés des sites évalués dans la zone de santé de Nyemba. L’éloignement constitue la principale barrière dans les trois sites (58%), suivi des frais de transport élevés (32%). Près de trois quarts des ménages (72%) doivent marcher plus d’une heure pour atteindre un marché.

• La plupart des commerçants ont constaté une augmentation de la demande depuis décembre 2025 et la majorité (81%) se dit en mesure d’absorber une hausse légère à modérée de leur volume d’affaires à l’avenir.

• Une intervention cash pourrait contribuer à réduire certaines barrières d’accès, notamment les coûts de transport et les contraintes d’abordabilité pour les menages évalués. Toutefois, les capacités d’absorption des marchés demeurent restreintes par des insuffisances de capital (76%), en particulier sur les sites approvisionnés par le marché de Lukombe, où ces contraintes sont exacerbées par une fonctionnalité limitée du marché, augmentant le risque de tensions sur les prix et sur la disponibilité des produits.

fields.title: Ebola Outbreak, DRC and Region, Situation Report #10, July 8, 2026
fields.body:
**FAST FACTS**

- On May 15, the DRC officially declared an outbreak of Ebola virus disease (EVD), later confirmed to be caused by the Bundibugyo strain.
- As of July 7, 1,708 confirmed EVD cases and 580 confirmed EVD deaths had been reported in the DRC, with 20 confirmed cases and two confirmed deaths in Uganda.
- On July 4, the Uganda Ministry of Health reported a positive case of Marburg, a hemorrhagic fever closely related to EVD.
- In the DRC, the outbreak remains centered in Ituri province, but cases have also been confirmed in Haut-Uele, North Kivu and South Kivu.
- In Uganda, reported cases remain linked to transmission originating in the DRC.
- In South Sudan, no EVD cases have been confirmed, but risk of an outbreak remains high due to transient populations and porous borders with the DRC.

**OUR RESPONSE**

- Across the region, our teams are providing case management, infection prevention and control, screening and triage, risk communication and community engagement, training and preparedness planning.
- We are supporting 89 facilities of different types in the screening, identification and treatment of EVD.
- Our supported sites have conducted 121,777 EVD screenings and provided treatment to 422 patients, including 132 confirmed cases.
- We have so far trained 2,208 people in the DRC on EVD case management, response and transmission prevention.

With the support of the US Department of State, DG ECHO and other donors, International Medical Corps teams are actively responding to the outbreak across the region and collaborating closely with ministries of health, key actors and response partners. International Medical Corps has used a hub-and-spoke model since the outbreak was declared to provide case-management support at designated treatment and transit facilities, strengthen IPC in referring health facilities, conduct screening and triage, support risk communication in surrounding communities and train health workers on EVD response protocols.

Across all operational areas, International Medical Corps continues to closely coordinate with the MoH and relevant partners to support case management, screening-and-referral units, facility-based surveillance, mental health, infection prevention and control, logistics, just-in-time training, continuity of essential health services, and water, sanitation and hygiene services. International Medical Corps also is part of broader efforts to expand contact tracing, helping to strengthen mapping, daily monitoring and follow-up activities to address the risk of undetected transmission across operational areas and beyond.

fields.title: Speech by Commissioner Lahbib at the European Parliament on the Ebola Outbreak in Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda
fields.body:
**"Check against delivery"**

The Ebola outbreak continues to expand rapidly in a region marked by conflict, displacement and fragile health systems.

It is a crisis within a crisis, and a reminder that in today's interconnected world, no country can face a major health threat alone. Sustained financing and global coordination are essential.

According to European Centre for Disease Control and Prevention (ECDC), the risk of infection for people living in the EU remains very low. But this is no reason for complacency. We remain vigilant, and the Health Security Committee issued an Opinion on the current recommendations for a common EU approach.

The EU and our Member States are prepared for an imported Ebola case, as France's rapid response has shown.

A few weeks ago, I travelled to Bunia, in Ituri, the epicentre of the outbreak, to show EU solidarity and reaffirm our support. I saw the extraordinary bravery of healthcare and humanitarian workers operating in extremely difficult conditions.

But I also saw that Europe's support matters. Our presence, our reliability and our ability to act quickly are recognised and highly valued by our partners. Our assistance is saving lives.

Major challenges remain. Our priority is to boost surveillance and contact tracing, deploy diagnostics and medical teams, support exit screening, keep humanitarian and health access open, strengthen community trust, and ensure respect for International Humanitarian Law.

Since day one, we have worked with WHO, the Africa CDC, affected countries and partners. European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control experts have been deployed through the EU Health Task Force to support Africa CDC and strengthen exit screening.

Overall, the EU is providing immediate and long-term support of EUR 493 million for humanitarian aid, vaccines, treatments and health security in the region.

This includes more than EUR 100 million for the current preparedness and response efforts, with EUR 16.5 million to rapidly deploy diagnostics and strengthen field testing capacities.

We have invested EUR 39.3 million in CEPI – the Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations – to support the development of new, broadly protective filovirus vaccines.

We have also mobilised EUR 15 million in humanitarian assistance for WHO and frontline humanitarian partners and transported over 590 tonnes of essential supplies to eastern DRC through 8 EU Humanitarian Air Bridges.

Our response builds on years of EU investment in health security in Africa: healthcare workforce training, public health institutes and laboratory capacities, clinical trials infrastructure, surveillance, logistics, and research into vaccines, therapeutics, and diagnostics.

Beyond our bilateral and Team Europe action, we continue to provide significant multilateral contributions to the global health initiatives such as the Pandemic Fund, Gavi and the Global Fund, which are mobilising flexible funding to help countries respond to Ebola and maintain essential health services.

While responding to the immediate crisis, we must also continue building long-term preparedness and response capacity. This is the aim of the Global Health Resilience Initiative: to help countries prevent, prepare for and respond to current and future outbreaks.

Containing Ebola requires solidarity, political commitment to bring a lasting solution to the conflict, sustained financing, and reliable access to lifesaving humanitarian supplies and medical equipment. I have pleaded for that already in February during my first visit to the Great Lakes region.

We must invest in what is needed today while preparing for tomorrow. This is why sustainable financing and health preparedness must go hand in hand in the next Multiannual Financial Framework.

You can count on my commitment to support the response, mobilise resources, and advocate for humanitarian access and respect for international humanitarian law.

source.name: European Commission
source.spanish_name: Comisión Europea

fields.title: Procédures De Dédouanement Des Intrants Humanitaires En RDC À L'attention Des Organisations Humanitaires Et De Leurs Transitaires
fields.body:
Il est rappelé que la législation douanière congolaise prévoit plusieurs mécanismes permettant d'éviter le blocage des intrants humanitaires et des marchandises de secours lorsque certains documents sont encore en attente.  
1\. Principe général  
L'absence temporaire de certains documents ne signifie pas automatiquement que les marchandises doivent rester bloquées en douane. Des procédures spécifiques existent pour permettre leur enlèvement sous contrôle de l'administration douanière.  
2\. Procédure d'enlèvement immédiat  
Base légale : Article 128 du Code des Douanes  
Cette disposition autorise l'enlèvement des marchandises avant l'accomplissement complet des formalités douanières lorsque l'urgence le justifie. Elle est applicable notamment aux intrants humanitaires, matériels de secours et biens nécessaires aux opérations d'urgence.  
3\. Déclaration simplifiée, provisoire ou incomplète  
Référence : Circulaire DGDA du 12 septembre 2015 - Point 4 :  
« L'enlèvement d'une marchandise peut se faire sur la base d'une déclaration simplifiée, provisoire ou incomplète sous réserve de l'autorisation de la douane. »  
Cette procédure permet de poursuivre le dédouanement même lorsque certains documents justificatifs ne sont pas encore disponibles.  
4\. Envois de secours  
Référence : Circulaire DGDA du 12 septembre 2015 – Point 6.b  
Les marchandises de secours peuvent être enlevées sur base d'une déclaration provisoire, même en l'absence de certains documents requis, avec dispense de garantie.  
Cette disposition vise spécifiquement à éviter tout retard dans l'assistance humanitaire.  
5\. Autorisation de la DGDA  
Référence : Circulaire DGDA du 12 septembre 2015 – Point 7  
La souscription d'une déclaration provisoire pour les marchandises visées au point 6 est subordonnée à l'autorisation du Directeur Général des Douanes et Accises ou de son délégué.  
En cas de difficulté au niveau opérationnel, les organisations peuvent solliciter l'intervention des autorités compétentes de la DGDA afin de faciliter l'application de ces dispositions.  
6\. Recommandations aux organisations  
• Vérifier avec votre transitaire qu'il connaît ces procédures.  
• Demander explicitement l'utilisation d'une déclaration simplifiée, provisoire ou incomplète lorsque les conditions sont réunies.  
• Pour les marchandises humanitaires et de secours, rappeler les dispositions prévues dans la circulaire du 12 septembre 2015.  
• En cas de blocage injustifié, solliciter des explications écrites et, si nécessaire, porter le dossier à l'attention de la hiérarchie de la DGDA.

fields.title: P21 - Chad | Monthly Report Border Monitoring (June 2026)
fields.body: A comparative analysis of border monitoring results between May and June 2026 highlights significant shifts in arrival patterns, displacement profiles, and protection risks. While overall trends confirm the continued predominance of security-related drivers, the data point to a reconfiguration of entry areas, a stabilization of displacement drivers, and a contrasting evolution of reported risks during displacement.

fields.title: P21 - Tchad | Rapport Mensuel Monitoring aux Frontières (Juin 2026)
fields.body: L’analyse comparative des résultats du monitoring aux frontières entre mai et juin 2026 met en évidence des évolutions notables dans les dynamiques d’arrivées, les profils de déplacement et les risques de protection. Si les tendances globales confirment la prédominance des facteurs sécuritaires, les données révèlent une reconfiguration des zones d’entrée, une stabilisation des motifs de déplacement et une évolution contrastée des risques déclarés au cours du déplacement.
